response: The internet traces its origins to 1960s U.S. Department of Defense-funded computer networking research, which produced ARPANET, the internet’s precursor, that successfully sent its first message between UCLA and Stanford Research Institute in 1969. Core foundational milestones that shaped the modern internet followed, including the 1983 transition to TCP/IP protocols that created the internet’s core technical framework, Tim Berners-Lee’s 1989 invention of the World Wide Web at CERN, and the 1993 launch of the Mosaic web browser, which first opened the web to ordinary non-technical users. The late 1990s dot-com boom brought massive investment in internet startups, though most collapsed in the 2001 dot-com crash, while surviving firms Amazon and Google grew into some of the world’s most valuable companies; subsequent 2000s milestones that expanded the internet’s reach and influence included the rise of social media platforms Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube, the 2006 launch of AWS that pioneered mainstream cloud computing, and the 2007 iPhone release that made ubiquitous mobile internet a reality. Today, the internet connects over 5 billion people globally, underpinning nearly every aspect of modern life from commerce and communication to entertainment.
